nodes.text: Mastering the Goethe & TELC A1 Writing Section

The writing task in the Goethe and TELC A1 exams proves you can communicate in everyday German. You'll complete a short functional task and compose a mini letter or email. With a clear structure, simple vocabulary, and the right greetings and endings, you can earn easy points and finish confidently.
Complete a form or note and write a short message (around 40–50 words).
Goethe A1: 20 minutes · TELC A1: 30 minutes for writing tasks.
Clear sentences, correct word order, and polite tone win more points than fancy words.

nodes.alt: Student planning Goethe and TELC A1 writing responses

nodes.text: How the Writing Section Is Structured
Task 1 · Forms or Short Notes (5–10 minutes)

items.text: Fill in personal information (name, address, phone) or write a short message.
Focus on spelling and capital letters for nouns.
Answer exactly what the prompt asks—no extra sentences needed.

nodes.text: Task 2 · Short Text or Letter (15–20 minutes)

items.text: Write 4–6 sentences (about 40–60 words) answering every bullet in the prompt.
Use present tense, simple past (Perfekt), and future (using werden) sparingly if requested.
Structure your answer with greeting, body (3–4 points), and a polite closing.
Greeting (formal or informal)
Purpose sentence
Answer each bullet (1 sentence each)
Friendly farewell + signature
Underline key instructions: name, date, place, invitation, response, etc.
Decide on formal vs informal tone before you start writing.
Draft sentence starters in the margin, then copy neatly to the answer sheet.
Keep verbs in second position: Ich komme morgen.
Use capital letters for nouns: die Party, die Einladung.
Connect ideas with simple words: und, aber, denn, oder, deshalb, weil.
Include at least one question in informal letters to keep the conversation natural.

nodes.text: Choosing the Right Tone: Informal vs Formal

nodes.alt: Example cue cards for Goethe and TELC A1 writing section

nodes.text: Used for friends, family, classmates, or roommates.

items.text: Hallo [Name],
Liebe [Name],
Lieber [Name],
Liebe Grüße,
Bis bald!
Viele Grüße,

nodes.text: Ich hoffe, es geht dir gut.
Use for offices, schools, landlords, course coordinators, or unknown recipients.

items.text: Sehr geehrte Damen und Herren,
Sehr geehrter Herr [Name],
Sehr geehrte Frau [Name],
Mit freundlichen Grüßen,
Hochachtungsvoll,
Vielen Dank und freundliche Grüße,

nodes.text: Ich möchte mich nach dem Deutschkurs erkundigen.
ich hatte am Samstag Geburtstag und mache eine kleine Feier zu Hause.
Die Party beginnt um 18 Uhr, und es gibt leckeres Essen. Bringst du bitte einen Salat mit?
Kannst du mir bis Freitag schreiben, ob du kommst?
Formal email: Ask for course details
ich interessiere mich für Ihren Deutschkurs A1 am Abend. Können Sie mir bitte Informationen zu den Terminen und den Kosten schicken?
Ich arbeite montags bis donnerstags. Gibt es einen Kurs am Wochenende?
Vielen Dank im Voraus für Ihre Antwort.

items.text: Skipping one of the bullet points in the task.
Mixing informal sentences with formal greetings.
Forgetting capital letters for nouns.
Verb in the wrong position: *Ich morgen komme* ❌
Tick off each bullet after you write the sentence.
Keep a list of polite phrases and learn the correct endings (du vs Sie).
Read your text aloud quietly. If it sounds natural, it probably is.
Check verbs last: subject + conjugated verb + rest.
Read the full task twice and underline key words.
Spend one minute planning your 3–4 body sentences.
Use connectors to join ideas but keep each sentence under 12 words.
Include one nice detail (time, place, reason) to sound natural.
Always finish with a closing phrase and your name.
Reserve 2 minutes to proofread spelling, verbs, and punctuation.

nodes.text: Boost Your Score with Focused Writing Practice
Apply these tips with our interactive Goethe & TELC A1 writing prompts. Get instant feedback on greetings, grammar, and tone so you know you're on track for test day.
Aim for 4–6 concise sentences. The examiners care more about covering all bullet points than hitting an exact word count.
Avoid them. Instead, rephrase using simpler German you know. Examiners cannot award points for English words.
Minor spelling errors are generally acceptable if the meaning stays clear. However, repeated mistakes or consistently missing capital letters for nouns can lower your score.

Both exams expect the same basics: task-focused answers, polite tone, and clear structure. TELC A1 sometimes gives two short writing prompts instead of one longer letter, but the scoring criteria stay similar.
You're Ready to Impress the Examiner

Consistency beats complexity at the A1 level. Practice writing short, purposeful messages, memorize your greetings and endings, and always double-check the task requirements. With these strategies, you can walk into the Goethe or TELC A1 exam confident that your writing section will deliver the points you need.
